claims.claim_text: Kubernetes does not ship built-in pod networking; you must install a compatible CNI plugin before pods can communicate.
claims.span_hint: lines 56, 572 | Did You Know; Quiz Q1 answer
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes network plugins page directly states that a CNI plugin is required to implement the Kubernetes network model.

claims.claim_text: The Kubernetes network model gives each Pod a unique cluster-wide IP, requires direct pod-to-pod communication without NAT, and allows node agents to reach pods on that node.
claims.rationale: The Services, Load Balancing, and Networking overview states these core networking-model properties explicitly.

claims.claim_text: The Kubernetes network model is implemented on each node by the container runtime, and common runtimes use CNI plugins for networking and security capabilities.
claims.span_hint: lines 33, 99-107 | Why This Module Matters; What CNI Provides
claims.rationale: The section pool source for Cluster Networking directly covers the implementation role of container runtimes and common use of CNI plugins.

claims.claim_text: Calico: Yes NetworkPolicy, High performance, Enterprise/security-focused.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Calico** | Yes | Depends on dataplane and workload | Networking and policy across clouds, VMs, and bare metal |
claims.rationale: Calico's policy support and deployment scope are sourceable, but the unqualified 'High' performance ranking is not anchored to an authoritative comparative benchmark.

claims.claim_text: Cilium: Yes (advanced), Very high performance, eBPF/observability use case.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Cilium** | Yes | Depends on workload | eBPF, observability, and policy |
claims.rationale: Cilium's eBPF, observability, and policy capabilities are sourceable, but 'Very high' is an uncited comparative performance claim.

claims.claim_text: Flannel: No NetworkPolicy, Medium performance, Simple clusters.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Flannel** | No built-in NetworkPolicy enforcement | Depends on backend | Simple overlay networking |
claims.rationale: The policy limitation is supportable, but the table's fixed performance label is not backed by a stable upstream benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: Canal combines Flannel networking with Calico policy.
claims.span_hint: line 155, line 578 | Part 2.1 comparison table; Quiz Q2 answer
claims.rationale: The primary current documentation for Canal is on Tigera's docs host, which is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: AWS VPC CNI: Via Calico, High performance, EKS native.
claims.anchor_text: | **AWS VPC CNI** | Via Calico | High | EKS native |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **AWS VPC CNI** | Yes in v1.14+ | Depends on mode and workload | EKS native |
claims.rationale: The current row is outdated because upstream AWS VPC CNI now documents native Kubernetes NetworkPolicy support in recent releases.

claims.claim_text: nftables is the default kube-proxy mode in modern clusters starting with Kubernetes v1.33+.
claims.anchor_text: | **nftables** | Uses nftables | Best | Default in modern clusters (k8s v1.33+) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **nftables** | Uses nftables | Better scalability in large clusters | Modern Linux clusters that explicitly enable it (stable since k8s v1.33) |
claims.rationale: Upstream docs mark nftables stable and enabled by default as a feature state, but also describe iptables as the default mode users migrate from.

claims.claim_text: IPVS proxy mode is deprecated in Kubernetes v1.35, had service edge-case gaps, and nftables is recommended as its replacement.
claims.rationale: The shared pool source for Virtual IPs and Service Proxies directly documents IPVS deprecation, edge-case mismatch, and the recommendation to prefer nftables.

claims.claim_text: userspace is a current kube-proxy mode and is deprecated.
claims.anchor_text: | **userspace** | Legacy, user-space proxy | Poor | Never use (deprecated) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **userspace** | Historical proxy mode | Obsolete | Avoid in new clusters |
claims.rationale: Current Kubernetes reference docs no longer list userspace among the available Linux kube-proxy modes, so the row should be reframed as historical guidance rather than a current mode entry.

claims.claim_text: kube-proxy watches Services and EndpointSlices and programs node rules so ClusterIP traffic is redirected to endpoints; in iptables mode it DNATs and selects a backend Pod at random by default.
claims.span_hint: lines 345-369, line 590 | Part 4.2; Quiz Q4 answer
claims.rationale: The shared pool source for Virtual IPs and Service Proxies explicitly describes kube-proxy watching Services and EndpointSlices and using packet-rewrite rules with random backend selection by default.

claims.claim_text: NetworkPolicy resources only take effect when the cluster uses a networking solution that supports NetworkPolicy enforcement; otherwise creating the resource has no effect.
claims.span_hint: line 467, line 578 | Common Network Issues table; Quiz Q2 answer
claims.rationale: The shared pool Network Policies source states this behavior directly, including that creating a NetworkPolicy without an implementing controller has no effect.

claims.claim_text: In kubeadm clusters, CoreDNS remains Pending until a pod network add-on is installed.
claims.rationale: The kubeadm troubleshooting page explicitly says CoreDNS Pending is expected until the pod network is set up.

claims.claim_text: Pods running with hostNetwork should explicitly set dnsPolicy to ClusterFirstWithHostNet; otherwise they fall back to Default DNS behavior.
claims.span_hint: line 562, line 596 | Common Mistakes; Quiz Q5 answer
claims.rationale: The shared pool DNS source states this exact fallback behavior for hostNetwork pods.

claims.claim_text: VXLAN uses UDP destination port 4789 by default.
claims.rationale: RFC 7348 explicitly assigns UDP destination port 4789 as the default VXLAN port.
RFC 4271 states that BGP uses TCP and listens on TCP port 179.

claims.claim_text: Cilium can fully replace kube-proxy and implement service load balancing in eBPF.
claims.rationale: The upstream Cilium repository README explicitly says Cilium can fully replace kube-proxy and implements distributed load balancing in eBPF.

further_reading.title: Cluster Networking
further_reading.why_relevant: Best Kubernetes-side overview of the network model, IP ranges, and where CNI fits.

further_reading.title: CNI Specification
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines the runtime-plugin contract, including ADD/DEL, namespaces, and attachment lifecycle.

further_reading.title: Virtual IPs and Service Proxies
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers kube-proxy behavior, EndpointSlice watching, and the current state of iptables, IPVS, and nftables modes.

notes: Skipped shell snippets, YAML examples, and ASCII diagrams unless the surrounding prose made a concrete factual claim. The largest concentration of salvage work is in the comparison tables, where stable capability claims are mixed with uncited comparative performance labels or outdated mode/version assertions.
